does: This review examines safety challenges posed by large language models and AI systems in medical applications. It categorizes risks into LLM-specific safety issues spanning data collection, model training, and deployment phases, as well as broader safety problems common to all AI systems that are compounded by LLM capabilities. The work addresses both functional concerns like hallucinations and model reliability, and communication challenges around transparency and user understanding. By systematically mapping emerging risks across the AI development pipeline and clinical implementation, the paper provides a comprehensive framework for understanding how powerful language models introduce novel safety obstacles while simultaneously amplifying existing AI safety concerns in healthcare contexts.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about AI safety in clinical settings that have emerged as adoption barriers, extending prior research on AI trustworthiness, transparency, and bias in medical AI systems. It responds to the specific surge in safety questions triggered by LLMs achieving superhuman performance on certain medical tasks and their novel interaction paradigm through natural language. The review synthesizes knowledge from broader AI safety literature, healthcare AI governance frameworks, and recent empirical findings about LLM failure modes like hallucination and inconsistency. It connects to ongoing discussions about responsible AI deployment in high-stakes domains and builds on earlier work examining data quality, model interpretability, and human-AI collaboration in medical practice.
enables: This work enables development of targeted safety frameworks and evaluation protocols specifically designed for LLM deployment in clinical contexts. It points toward future research on detecting and mitigating LLM-specific failure modes in medical tasks, improved validation methodologies for medical AI systems, and better communication tools to help clinicians understand model limitations and appropriate use cases. The comprehensive risk taxonomy supports the creation of regulatory guidelines and clinical governance structures for safe LLM integration into healthcare workflows. It also enables more informed discussions between AI developers, medical practitioners, and policymakers about acceptable safety thresholds and enables design of human-in-the-loop systems that leverage LLM capabilities while maintaining clinical oversight and accountability.
